name: Proctor, Robert W.
recent_1: Zhang, C., Tian, C., Han, T., Li, H., Feng, Y., Chen, Y., Proctor, R. W., & Zhang, J. (2025). Evaluation of an Infrastructure-Based Warning System: A Case Study on Roundabout Driving Behaviors. IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems, 26(5), 6056-6069. DOI: 10.1109/TITS.2025.3548508
recent_1_synopsis: This case study evaluated an infrastructure-based (roadside) warning system intended to support safer driver behavior when approaching and negotiating roundabouts, assessing how the warnings shaped speed and maneuvering. It reflects Proctor's recent applied work on connected/automated transportation infrastructure and human-centered warning design. It matters because it provides empirical evidence on how externally delivered warnings change real driving behavior at a high-conflict road geometry.
recent_2: Proctor, R. W., & Hung, Y.-H. (2024). Sensation and Perception in HCI. In Human-Computer Interaction (Vol. 1, pp. 55-86). CRC Press. DOI: 10.1201/9781003584292-2
recent_2_synopsis: This book chapter synthesizes how human sensory and perceptual processing constrains and informs the design of human-computer interfaces, translating basic perception science into design-relevant principles. It exemplifies Proctor's long-standing role in bridging cognitive psychology and HCI/human factors pedagogy. It matters as an authoritative reference that grounds interface design in well-established perceptual mechanisms.
recent_3: Liu, W., Cao, Y., Liang, X., Proctor, R. W., & Duffy, V. G. (2024). Facing a Trend of Icon Simplicity: Evidence from Event-Related Potentials. International Journal of Human-Computer Interaction, 40(4), 945-955. DOI: 10.1080/10447318.2023.2169978
recent_3_synopsis: Using event-related potentials (ERPs) alongside behavioral measures, this study examined how varying levels of visual simplicity in interface icons affect recognition and the neural correlates of icon processing. It connects Proctor's interest in perception-action and information processing to contemporary visual interface design. It matters because it offers neurophysiological evidence to guide icon design choices in an era of increasingly minimalist visual interfaces.
seminal_1: Lu, C.-H., & Proctor, R. W. (1995). The influence of irrelevant location information on performance: A review of the Simon and spatial Stroop effects. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 2(2), 174-207. DOI: 10.3758/BF03210959
seminal_1_synopsis: This review synthesized empirical findings and theoretical accounts of how task-irrelevant spatial location information interferes with choice responses, organizing the Simon and spatial Stroop literatures into a coherent framework. With approximately 1,340 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is Proctor's most-cited work and is foundational because it established the conceptual structure and terminology that have guided decades of research on spatial stimulus-response correspondence and selective attention.
seminal_2: Proctor, R. W., & Cho, Y. S. (2006). Polarity correspondence: A general principle for performance of speeded binary classification tasks. Psychological Bulletin, 132(3), 416-442. DOI: 10.1037/0033-2909.132.3.416
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper advanced the polarity-correspondence principle, proposing that stimulus and response alternatives in binary tasks are coded asymmetrically as positive/negative polarities and that performance is best when corresponding polarities are paired. With approximately 820 citations (Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17), it is foundational because it offered a unifying, parsimonious account spanning orthogonal stimulus-response compatibility, the Implicit Association Test, and numerical (e.g., SNARC) judgments, and was published in a top-tier review outlet (Psychological Bulletin).
expertise_overview: Proctor studies the relationship between perception and action, with a central focus on stimulus-response compatibility (SRC), spatial correspondence phenomena such as the Simon effect and spatial Stroop effect, and the mechanisms of response selection. He develops theory on how stimuli and responses are coded (e.g., polarity correspondence, ideomotor/action-effect accounts) and how attention, practice, and skill acquisition shape choice-reaction performance. Methodologically he combines controlled choice-reaction-time experiments, meta-analytic and integrative review, and increasingly neurophysiological measures such as event-related potentials. His applied work translates basic cognitive principles into human factors and HCI design, including warnings, interface/icon design, and trust- and risk-related behavior. A prominent recent strand addresses the human factors of cybersecurity, especially phishing-webpage detection, training embedded in warnings, and risk perception in mobile application selection. He also works on human-centered design of connected and automated transportation systems.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence as Robert W. Proctor, Distinguished Professor of Psychological Sciences at Purdue University (research on perception-action, stimulus-response compatibility, attention/skill, and human factors of cybersecurity). Sources confirming identity: his Google Scholar profile (scholar.google.com/citations?user=_RhK-icAAAAJ), the Purdue/CERIAS news announcement of his Distinguished Professor title, and the HFES Fellows profile (rproctor@purdue.edu); the four pre-verified Human Factors phishing/SRC papers served as ground-truth anchors, disambiguating him from other people named Proctor. Citation counts are from Google Scholar, retrieved 2026-06-17: total ~25,400 citations, h-index ~74; seminal_1 (Lu & Proctor, 1995) ~1,340; seminal_2 (Proctor & Cho, 2006) ~820. Citation counts are approximate and may drift. recent_3 (icon simplicity) carries a 2023 online-first issue date in Crossref but appears in 2024 volume 40(4) and is retained as a recent peer-reviewed work; recent_2 is a 2024 book chapter (Proctor is the lead author). I declined to use a 2024 'A Tale of Two Distrusts' paper that appeared on his Scholar profile because the Crossref author list did not include Proctor. No DOIs were invented. All 8 DOIs cited in this profile (4 pre-verified Human Factors anchors plus 4 newly added recent/seminal DOIs) were verified via the Crossref API on 2026-06-17 and returned HTTP 200 with matching titles and Proctor confirmed in the author list where applicable. No unverifiable citations remain.
